title: Cutting the cost of sending money home: Fast payment systems, digital access, and the future of remittances
gist: New World Bank analysis shows global remittances reached an estimated $856 billion in 2024 ($653B to low- and middle-income countries, exceeding FDI and ODA in many developing economies) and that digital delivery has cut average costs to about 4% versus 7% for cash, while a domestic Fast Payment System is shown to cut a $200 transfer's cost by 0.3-1 percentage point — an immediately actionable policy lever the authors argue governments can pull now, ahead of remittances' larger potential as a credit-history and financial-inclusion gateway.

insights.claim: Global remittance flows reached an estimated $856 billion in 2024, with $653 billion flowing to low- and middle-income countries, and in many developing economies remittances now exceed both foreign direct investment and official development assistance.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Remittances now outrank FDI and aid as an external financing source for many developing economies — a structural macro fact relevant to any emerging-market balance-of-payments or currency-stability analysis.
insights.quote: remittances now exceed both foreign direct investment and official development assistance

insights.claim: Geopolitical developments, economic fragmentation, and disruptions to cross-border payment arrangements can affect not only remittance costs but whether migrants and their families can access reliable digital channels at all, making resilient payment ecosystems an increasingly important source of household resilience.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Flags cross-border payment fragmentation (sanctions, de-risking, correspondent-bank withdrawal) as a live tail risk to remittance-dependent households and economies — a channel to monitor amid rising geopolitical fragmentation.
insights.locator: But this potential is not yet available to everyone

themes.theme: The digital cost decline is real but uneven

insights.claim: The average cost of sending remittances fell from more than 9% in 2009 to around 6% today; digital remittances now average about 4% versus about 7% for cash-based services, and nearly half of all corridors are below 5%, though Sub-Saharan Africa still records the highest regional average cost.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: The digital-vs-cash cost gap (4% vs 7%) is a direct, quantifiable opportunity for fintech remittance providers, especially in Sub-Saharan Africa where costs remain highest.
insights.quote: the cost of digital remittances averaging around 4%, compared with around 7% for cash-based services
insights.locator: The Digital Revolution Is Delivering, But Unevenly

insights.claim: Senders with a smartphone, verified digital identity, and a linked account can access remittance services at more affordable rates, while those relying on cash due to limited connectivity, documentation constraints, or unfamiliarity with digital tools face significantly higher costs — an intra-corridor digital divide now as consequential as cross-corridor cost differences.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Identifies within-corridor access inequality, not just country-to-country cost differences, as an emerging policy priority — relevant for financial-inclusion-linked fintech and digital-ID investment theses.
insights.quote: Closing this intra-corridor digital divide is now as important as reducing differences across corridors themselves.

insights.claim: New research shows that the mere existence of a domestic Fast Payment System in a sending or receiving country reduces the cost of a $200 remittance by between 0.3 and 1 percentage point, primarily by letting non-bank providers bypass the costly correspondent banking chain.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A specific, measurable, immediately actionable lever — regulators granting remittance providers fair access to existing domestic Fast Payment Systems (no need to wait for cross-border interlinking) is a near-term, low-cost policy win worth tracking country by country.
insights.quote: the mere existence of a domestic Fast Payment System (FPS) in a sending or receiving country reduces the cost of a $200 remittance by between 0.3 and 1 percentage point
insights.locator: Ensure digital remittance providers can access domestic fast payment systems

insights.claim: The authors' three-lever agenda to close remittance cost gaps before 2030 is: fair non-bank access to domestic Fast Payment Systems, financial literacy investment paired with fixing rigid customer due-diligence rules that lock undocumented migrants into cash channels, and increased competition through a level regulatory playing field for banks and non-bank providers.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: A concrete policy checklist for evaluating whether a given remittance corridor or country is on track to close its cost gap — a useful screen for remittance-fintech regulatory-risk assessment.
insights.quote: Rigid customer due diligence requirements that lock undocumented migrants into cash channels must be addressed.

insights.claim: Remittance payment history could serve as a substitute for formal credit history, unlocking small productive loans for recipients who otherwise have none, while savings products and bundled health or life insurance linked to remittance accounts could extend financial protection using existing infrastructure.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Outlines a specific product roadmap — remittance-linked credit scoring, savings, and insurance — for fintechs and development finance institutions to build atop existing remittance rails rather than new infrastructure.
insights.quote: Remittance history, as a documented record of regular income, could serve as the basis for credit scoring in markets where recipients have no formal credit history

insights.claim: With 1.2 billion young people entering the workforce in developing countries over the next decade, linking remittances to savings, credit histories, and small-business financing is framed as part of the solution for seeding entrepreneurship and growing micro and small enterprises.
insights.speaker: World Bank Blogs
insights.so_what: Ties the remittance-inclusion agenda directly to the jobs problem — a long-horizon thesis linking fintech remittance products to informal-sector job creation in developing countries.
insights.quote: For the 1.2 billion young people entering the workforce in developing countries over the next decade, harnessing remittance flows to seed entrepreneurship and grow micro and small enterprises could be part of the answer.
